pillars.name: Strategy, Value & Portfolio
pillars.purpose: Ensure AI initiatives are anchored in a clear strategy, generate measurable business value, and are governed as a portfolio rather than a series of disconnected pilots. This pillar covers the AI vision, use-case discovery and prioritisation, business case development, portfolio governance (the "use-case factory"), benefits realisation, and continuous strategic reassessment.

pillars.name: Governance, Risk & Compliance
pillars.purpose: Establish the AI governance backbone: policies, accountable roles, AI risk management system, regulatory conformity (EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001), AI system inventory and lifecycle decisions, and third-party AI governance. This pillar makes AI risk treatable, auditable, and defensible to regulators and boards.

pillars.name: Responsible AI & Trust
pillars.purpose: Ensure AI systems are fair, transparent, privacy-preserving, ethically overseen, and aligned with human and societal well-being. This pillar covers fairness and non-discrimination, transparency and explainability, privacy and data protection, human oversight and recourse, stakeholder engagement, and sustainability of AI workloads. Security and adversarial defence are intentionally separated into Pillar 4.

pillars.name: AI Security & Assurance
pillars.purpose: Treat AI security as a distinct discipline. Cover threat modelling and secure MLOps, adversarial robustness and red-teaming, GenAI-specific safety (prompt injection, hallucination, leakage, jailbreak), model supply-chain integrity, post-market monitoring and incident response, and runtime guardrails with rollback. Aligned with OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025), MITRE ATLAS, NIST AI 100-2 (Adversarial ML), and EU AI Act Articles 12, 15, 72, 73.

pillars.name: Data & Knowledge
pillars.purpose: Ensure data and knowledge assets are fit for AI: quality, lineage, governance, scalable infrastructure, and the GenAI-specific knowledge layer (vectors, embeddings, RAG corpora, synthetic data). Combines classical DAMA-DMBoK discipline with the unstructured-data readiness that modern GenAI deployments demand.

pillars.name: Technology & AI Platforms
pillars.purpose: Build and operate the AI platform stack — compute, MLOps, LLMOps, agent platforms, model lifecycle, observability, integration, and resilience — so that AI systems can be developed, deployed, monitored, and evolved reliably at scale.

pillars.name: People, Literacy & Operating Model
pillars.purpose: Build the human and organisational capacity to deliver AI: a workforce-wide AI literacy programme (a legal obligation under EU AI Act Article 4), specialist talent strategy, an effective AI Center of Excellence and operating model, human-AI collaboration design, and partner and ecosystem engagement.

pillars.name: Adoption & Change Management
pillars.purpose: Drive AI adoption so that capability translates into outcomes. Cover executive sponsorship and narrative, workflow and process redesign, training rollout and enablement, communication and community, resistance management, and adoption metrics with feedback loops. Grounded in Prosci ADKAR and McKinsey/BCG empirical findings that adoption — not technology — separates AI leaders from laggards.

pillars.name: AI Value Delivery (BABOK-aligned)
pillars.purpose: Deliver AI value end-to-end through BABOK v3 disciplines applied to AI initiatives — discovery, elicitation, requirements, design, pilot, benefits realisation, handover. Distinct from Pillar 1 (which governs the portfolio) and Pillar 8 (which governs adoption); Pillar 9 governs how each use case progresses from idea to sustained value.

rau_rule.description: Racing Ahead Unsafely: capability outruns risk maturity.

healthy_rule.description: Healthy: high capability with strong risk control.

questions.what_it_covers: The organisation's articulated AI ambition, its alignment with corporate strategy, and the executive narrative that connects AI investment to measurable business outcomes.
questions.why_it_matters: McKinsey 2024 finds that AI leaders have a single, executive-owned AI narrative tied to two or three strategic priorities; laggards have many competing narratives. Without strategic alignment, AI spend fragments.
common_pitfalls: Vision so broad it justifies any project ("become AI-first") rather than guiding trade-offs.
Vision owned by IT alone; missing CFO or business-line accountability.
Vision never refreshed after publication; becomes shelfware in 6 months.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "AI Vision & Strategic Alignment"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: No documented AI vision. AI ambition exists in slide decks but is not connected to strategy.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Draft AI vision document exists. Communicated occasionally; not part of strategic planning cycle.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Published AI vision, approved by the executive team, mapped to 2–3 corporate strategic priorities. Refreshed annually.

options.label: Managed
options.description: AI vision drives the planning cycle. Funding allocation, KPI scorecards, and incentives are aligned to it. Reviewed quarterly.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: AI strategy is indistinguishable from corporate strategy. Continuously refreshed against market and capability benchmarks; communicated externally.

questions.what_it_covers: A repeatable process to surface candidate AI use cases from the business and rank them on impact, feasibility, risk, and strategic fit.
questions.why_it_matters: Most organisations never run out of AI ideas; they run out of the discipline to pick the right ones. A scored prioritisation framework is what separates portfolios from pilot factories.
common_pitfalls: Scoring rubric never validated against actual outcomes.
Prioritisation gated by a single executive's preference, not the rubric.
Risk class checked only after scoping is complete, leading to rework.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "Use-Case Discovery & Prioritization"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: Use cases surface ad-hoc from individual enthusiasm; no intake.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Periodic workshops collect ideas; informal ranking by 1–2 people.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Documented intake; scoring rubric covering impact, feasibility, risk, strategic fit. Cross-functional review committee.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Quarterly portfolio review. Scoring rubric calibrated against past outcomes. Risk-class screening integrated upstream.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Continuous, AI-assisted opportunity discovery from internal data. Scoring rubric tested for predictive validity against realised benefits.

questions.what_it_covers: Structured business cases for AI initiatives including benefits, costs, risks, assumptions, and explicit success criteria.
questions.why_it_matters: BCG 2024: 70% of AI value comes from people and process change. A credible business case must address operating-model change, not just technology cost. Without explicit success criteria, benefits cannot be tracked.
common_pitfalls: Benefits stated as "improved customer experience" without a measurable proxy.
Cost-of-ownership ignores model retraining, monitoring, and operations.
No explicit success criteria; impossible to confirm or deny benefits later.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "Business Case & Investment Planning"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: Business cases are narrative slides without quantified benefits or costs.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Templated business cases used for some initiatives; quality varies.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Standard business-case template required for any AI initiative ≥ defined cost threshold. Includes quantified benefits, costs, risks, success criteria, and sponsor sign-off.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Business cases integrate with portfolio register. Assumptions logged for later validation. Sensitivity analysis required.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Business cases are continuous, updated quarterly against actuals. AI-assisted forecasting calibrates assumptions across the portfolio.

questions.what_it_covers: The operating model that runs the AI portfolio end-to-end: intake, screening, pilot, scale, retire. Includes cadence, gates, owners, and capacity management.
questions.why_it_matters: Gartner and McKinsey both call the use-case factory a top-3 differentiator for AI leaders. Treating each use case as a one-off project produces duplicated effort, no learning compounding, and no capacity discipline.
common_pitfalls: Factory designed but never enforced; teams bypass intake.
Stage gates demand the same artefacts regardless of use-case size; over-engineering for small bets.
No retirement decisions; portfolio bloats with maintenance debt.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "Portfolio Governance (Use-Case Factory)"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: No portfolio view of AI work. Each initiative is independent.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Informal portfolio list maintained by one team. No gates or cadence.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Documented stage-gate factory (intake → screen → pilot → scale → retire) with named owners, defined gate criteria, monthly cadence.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Portfolio review at executive level. Capacity managed against demand. Cross-use-case patterns reused (data products, components). Retirement reviewed.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Self-service intake; portfolio dashboards in real time; AI-assisted matching of new ideas to reusable components and prior learnings.

questions.what_it_covers: Disciplined tracking of whether deployed AI systems deliver the benefits claimed in their business cases; visible accountability for realised value, not deployment milestones.
questions.why_it_matters: Only 11% of organisations capture meaningful EBIT impact from AI (McKinsey 2024); the differentiator is whether benefits are tracked against the business case after deployment, not before.
common_pitfalls: Benefits defined as activity (e.g., users interacting with the model) rather than business outcome.
Attribution missing; cannot tell whether realised gains came from AI or other changes.
No mechanism to retire underperforming systems; sunk-cost paralysis.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "Benefits Realisation & ROI Tracking"?

options.label: Developing

options.label: Defined

options.label: Managed
options.description: Quarterly benefits dashboard at executive level. Underperformers trigger rescoping or retirement. Aggregate portfolio value tracked.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Real-time benefits telemetry per use case; AI-assisted attribution; benefits realisation predicts and influences future investment.

questions.dimension_name: RACI, Decision Rights & AI Ethics Body
questions.what_it_covers: Defined accountable, responsible, consulted, and informed parties for AI decisions; existence and empowerment of an AI ethics body or officer with authority to halt launches and arbitrate trade-offs.

questions.dimension_name: EU AI Act Conformity (Risk Classification, FRIA, Technical Documentation)
questions.what_it_covers: Operational readiness for EU AI Act obligations: classifying each AI system under Article 6/7, completing FRIAs (Article 27) where required, producing technical documentation per Article 11/Annex IV, and applying conformity assessment per Article 43.
questions.why_it_matters: EU AI Act applies in phases starting February 2025; high-risk obligations effective August 2026. Penalties under Article 99 reach EUR 35M or 7% global turnover for prohibited-practice violations. Conformity is a hard gate for any organisation serving the EU market.
common_pitfalls: Treating risk classification as a one-time exercise; not refreshed when use cases evolve.
FRIA template adopted but not actually integrated with deployment gates.
Technical documentation accumulates inconsistently across teams.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "EU AI Act Conformity (Risk Classification, FRIA, Technical Documentation)"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: No mapping of AI portfolio to EU AI Act categories.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Awareness exercise complete; high-risk candidates identified at a high level.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Every in-scope AI system classified; FRIA template adopted; technical-documentation template per Annex IV in use for high-risk systems.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Pre-deployment conformity gate enforced for high-risk systems; FRIA completed and reviewed for every applicable deployer use case; technical documentation maintained and versioned.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Continuous conformity monitoring; system inventory reconciles automatically with classification; ready-state evidence packs producible on demand.

questions.what_it_covers: A complete, current inventory of AI systems in development and production, with ownership, risk class, purpose, status, and end-of-life decisions (per NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.6 and 1.7).
questions.why_it_matters: You cannot govern what you cannot see. ISO 42001 and NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.6 both require an AI system inventory. Without it, FRIAs, risk reviews, and conformity assessments are impossible at scale.
common_pitfalls: Spreadsheet inventory drifts out of date within months.
Inventory does not include third-party AI services or embedded vendor AI.
No decommissioning entries; retired systems clutter the register.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Inventory automated through telemetry; integrated with procurement, change management, and risk register; decommissioning workflow live.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Inventory updates in real time from CI/CD; inventory drives compliance evidence generation; AI-assisted detection of unregistered AI use.

questions.what_it_covers: Governance of AI supplied by third parties — model providers, embedded AI features in SaaS, open-source models, fine-tuners, annotation vendors. Includes contract clauses, risk assessment, evidence collection, and right-to-audit (ISO 42001 A.10; NIST AI RMF GOVERN 6.1, 6.2).
questions.why_it_matters: Most AI risk now enters via the supply chain — embedded LLM features, third-party fine-tunes, or upstream training data with unclear provenance. Without governance, organisations carry liability for risks they cannot see.
common_pitfalls: Treating embedded AI features in SaaS as ordinary software.
Open-source model use with no licence or provenance review.
No contingency for supplier withdrawal or model deprecation.

options.label: Optimizing

questions.what_it_covers: Dataset representativeness and diversity, fairness testing during development, post-deployment fairness drift monitoring, exception governance, and recourse for adverse automated decisions.
questions.why_it_matters: EU AI Act Article 10 requires bias-relevant data governance for high-risk systems; NIST AI RMF MEASURE 2.11 mandates fairness evaluation; discrimination lawsuits and reputational harm are leading AI risks (Stanford AI Index 2025).
common_pitfalls: Single fairness metric optimised in isolation (improving one harms another).
Bias tested only at training; not monitored post-deployment.
Protected attributes removed naively, leaving proxies that still discriminate.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Documented fairness test battery (e.g., disparate impact, equal opportunity, TPR parity) for high-risk systems; thresholds defined; results archived.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Fairness tests integrated into MLOps gates; post-deployment drift monitoring; exception workflow; user-recourse mechanism with SLAs.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Continuous fairness monitoring with auto-rollback; published fairness reports; external review; fairness goals inform model selection upstream.

questions.dimension_name: Model Supply-Chain Integrity (incl. Vector & Embedding Security)
questions.what_it_covers: Provenance and integrity of models, datasets, embeddings, plug-ins, and tools used by AI systems (OWASP LLM03 Supply Chain, LLM04 Data Poisoning, LLM06 Excessive Agency, LLM08 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses, NIST AI 600-1 Value Chain).
questions.why_it_matters: Most AI risk now enters via the supply chain — third-party models, fine-tunes, embeddings, training data. Without provenance, integrity cannot be asserted; without integrity, downstream attacks are undetectable.
common_pitfalls: Treating open-source model weights as inherently trustworthy.
Embeddings shared across tenants without isolation testing.
Agent given broad tool access; excessive agency exposes the entire backend.

questions.what_it_covers: Curation, indexing, and freshness of unstructured corpora powering Retrieval-Augmented Generation; embedding model selection and versioning; retrieval quality monitoring; vector store hygiene.

questions.dimension_name: AI Literacy Programme (EU AI Act Art. 4)
questions.what_it_covers: A workforce-wide AI literacy programme covering AI concepts, capabilities, limitations, risks, and responsible-use behaviours, differentiated by role (executives, managers, knowledge workers, customer-facing staff).
questions.why_it_matters: EU AI Act Article 4 (effective 2 February 2025) makes AI literacy a legal obligation for providers and deployers. It is distinct from specialist training. Without measurable literacy, an organisation cannot demonstrate compliance and cannot trust staff using AI.
common_pitfalls: Treating literacy as "what is a neural network" rather than safe use of AI in the actual workflow.
Single course for all roles; sales reps and ML engineers do not need the same content.
Completion tracked but comprehension not measured.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "AI Literacy Programme (EU AI Act Art. 4)"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: No literacy programme; AI knowledge is incidental.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Optional courses available; uneven uptake; no tracking.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Mandatory role-based AI literacy curriculum for in-scope staff; completion tracked; refresh cadence defined.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Curriculum continuously updated; integrated into onboarding; comprehension assessed; manager attestation.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Literacy levels measured against outcomes (incident reduction, productive AI use); content shared with partners or industry.

questions.why_it_matters: NIST AI 600-1 names Human-AI Configuration as a primary GenAI risk. Poorly designed collaboration produces automation bias (over-trust) or counter-trust (ignoring AI entirely); both waste investment.

Treating "human-in-the-loop" as a checkbox without time, training, or authority.

questions.what_it_covers: Deliberate redesign of business processes to use AI well — not bolted onto existing processes — including hand-offs, decision points, quality checks, and exception handling.
questions.why_it_matters: BCG's 10/20/70 rule: only 10% of AI value comes from algorithms; 70% from people and process change. Bolted-on AI rarely delivers benefits even when the model is excellent.
common_pitfalls: Lift-and-shift — same process, plus an AI step at the end.
Process designed for the AI's strengths; users left to handle weaknesses.
No measurement of redesigned process versus baseline.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "Workflow & Process Redesign"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: AI added to existing process; no redesign.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Some processes redesigned for AI on an ad-hoc basis.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Documented process-redesign method applied to AI-impacted workflows; user representatives involved; outcomes measured.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Redesign is standard practice before deployment; continuous improvement; cross-process patterns reused.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Process redesign treated as core capability; AI and operating-model design co-evolve.

questions.what_it_covers: The first BABOK-aligned stage of an AI use case: framing the problem, current-state and future-state assessment, root-cause analysis, opportunity sizing, change-strategy options, and a draft business case.

No measurable rejection rate; everything advances.
Discovery outputs are slides, not artefacts that feed requirements.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Discovery is a recognised, valued capability; alumni network of past discoveries influences future framing.

questions.why_it_matters: NIST AI RMF MAP 1.6 requires that requirements be elicited and understood from a wide range of AI actors. EU AI Act FRIA (Art. 27) expects engagement with affected persons. Weak elicitation is a leading cause of acceptance failure.
common_pitfalls: Elicitation only of business sponsors; end users absent.
Workshops generate quotes, not structured requirements.
Affected groups consulted after design is fixed.
questions.prompt: Provide a recent elicitation plan, stakeholder register, and traceability from elicitation outputs to requirements.

questions.what_it_covers: Functional, data, non-functional (including AI-specific NFRs: fairness, explainability, latency, robustness, energy), ethics, and compliance requirements; acceptance and evaluation criteria; requirements traceability.
questions.why_it_matters: AI NFRs are absent from most teams' default templates yet drive most AI risk. EU AI Act Articles 9–15 require documented requirements for high-risk systems. Without explicit acceptance criteria, pilots cannot be objectively evaluated.
common_pitfalls: Use AI stated as a requirement; no functional or non-functional specifics.
Acceptance criteria written in slides, not test-ready form.
No update to requirements when scope shifts; traceability lost.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "Requirements & Acceptance Criteria"?

options.label: Initial

options.description: Standard requirements set including AI-specific NFRs (fairness, explainability, latency, robustness, energy); acceptance criteria explicit; traceability to design and tests.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Requirements managed across the lifecycle; change control; traceability to operations; reused requirements catalog.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Requirements are product-grade: testable, traceable, versioned, and reused; AI-specific NFRs benchmarked externally.

questions.what_it_covers: Solution architecture (model, data, integration), explicit evaluation plan (metrics, datasets, thresholds, holdouts), prototyping, and cross-pillar control mapping (security, fairness, oversight).
questions.why_it_matters: Solution design that omits the evaluation plan upfront produces pilots that cannot be objectively judged. BABOK RADD demands evaluation criteria as part of design definition, not as an afterthought.
common_pitfalls: Evaluation criteria defined after the model is trained.
Evaluation dataset reuses training data; metrics inflated.
No mapping of design to required controls; gaps surface in production.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "Solution Design & Evaluation Criteria"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: Solution chosen ad hoc; evaluation improvised.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Architecture documented; evaluation criteria thin.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Documented design (architecture, data, integration); evaluation plan with metrics, datasets, thresholds, holdouts; controls mapped to P3/P4.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Design reviews include cross-pillar leads; prototypes inform design choices; evaluation outcomes archived.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Design and evaluation patterns reused; architecture choices benchmarked; design contributes back to platform standards.

questions.what_it_covers: The sequence from pilot through production — gating decisions, expected evidence at each gate, integration with security (P4), platform deployment (P6), and adoption (P8).
questions.why_it_matters: Pilots that linger as pilots forever produce no business value. Pilots that promote without gates produce risk surprises. The lifecycle must be explicit and enforced.
common_pitfalls: Pilots become permanent without ever crossing a production gate.
Gate decisions made by a single owner without cross-pillar review.
No retirement gate; legacy AI accumulates.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "Pilot-to-Production Lifecycle"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: No defined lifecycle; pilots linger or graduate informally.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Lifecycle drafted; gates inconsistent.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Documented pilot-to-production lifecycle with named gates and required evidence per gate; integrates with P4, P6, P8 controls.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Gate decisions tracked; gate failures cause rescoping or retirement; lifecycle metrics monitored.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Lifecycle continuously improved with cohort data; templated for use-case classes.

questions.what_it_covers: Post-launch evaluation against business case, identification of limitations and unintended consequences, decision to continue, rescope, or retire (BABOK Solution Evaluation), and capture of lessons learned.
questions.why_it_matters: Most organisations celebrate launch; only AI leaders measure the realisation curve and act when it dips. BABOK Solution Evaluation provides the discipline; AI's value depends on applying it.
common_pitfalls: Benefits review skipped because "we already know it works."
Lessons learned captured but never referenced.
No retirement decisions; underperformers consume platform capacity.
questions.prompt: Which option best describes your organisation's CURRENT practice on "Benefits Realisation & Continuous Improvement"?

options.label: Initial
options.description: No post-launch evaluation.

options.label: Developing
options.description: Ad-hoc reviews of selected systems.

options.label: Defined
options.description: Mandatory benefits review per use case at 90 days and 12 months post-launch; outcomes recorded; lessons learned captured.

options.label: Managed
options.description: Realisation curve tracked monthly; underperformers rescoped or retired; lessons systematically fed back to design and discovery.

options.label: Optimizing
options.description: Continuous, automated benefits telemetry; closed loop into use-case ideation and design; benefits attribution rigorous.
